name: Carpenter v. United States
summary: The government obtained 127 days of historical cell-site location information (CSLI) from a wireless carrier without a warrant, using the data to place a robbery suspect near crime scenes. The Court held that the third-party doctrine does not extend to comprehensive digital location records that reveal the privacies of daily life.
holding: The government must obtain a warrant supported by probable cause before accessing historical CSLI records. The third-party doctrine does not apply to the long-term tracking of a person's movements through carrier-held CSLI because such records are not truly voluntarily disclosed and create a comprehensive chronicle of the individual's life.

legal_definitions.definition: Time-stamped records generated each time a mobile phone connects to a cell tower, which when aggregated provide a detailed chronicle of an individual's past physical movements.
legal_definitions.context: The Court explained that CSLI differs from ordinary business records because individuals do not truly voluntarily share it and it enables near-perfect retrospective surveillance.

relevance_to_audit: Limits the third-party doctrine for comprehensive digital location data; directly relevant to government acquisition of ALPR data, cellular tracking, and other persistent location surveillance programs.

key_quotes.text: When the Government tracks the location of a cell phone it achieves near perfect surveillance, as if it had attached an ankle monitor to the phone's user.
key_quotes.context: Explaining why long-term CSLI collection is categorically different from ordinary third-party business records.

procedural_posture: Sixth Circuit affirmed; reversed and remanded
subsequent_treatment: Leading digital-privacy precedent; applied to ALPR data, tower dumps, and other comprehensive surveillance records by lower courts
